content.text: Out-of-Core Processing: Handling Data Larger Than Memory in Python

When a dataset is too large to fit into RAM, the typical in-memory approach of loading everything at once collapses. Out-of-core processing (also called external memory processing) is the discipline of working with data that lives on disk and is brought into memory only in small, manageable pieces вҖ” allowing Python programs to process datasets that are gigabytes, terabytes, or even petabytes in size on ordinary hardware.

Out-of-core processing refers to any computational strategy where the data being processed does not reside entirely in main memory (RAM) at any one time. Instead, data is streamed or chunked from persistent storage (disk, network, object store), processed incrementally, and results are written back out вҖ” all while keeping the memory footprint bounded and predictable.
The name comes from older computing terminology where "core" referred to magnetic core memory. Today it simply means "outside of RAM."
Modern datasets routinely exceed available RAM:
A CSV of 500 million rows might be 80 GB on disk вҖ” impossible to
in one shot on a 16 GB laptop.
A machine learning feature matrix with 10 billion samples cannot fit in memory even on large cloud VMs.
Log files, sensor streams, genomic datasets, and financial tick data are inherently unbounded.

The alternative вҖ” buying more RAM вҖ” is expensive, temporary, and doesn't scale indefinitely. Out-of-core processing makes it possible to run real workloads on realistic hardware, and it is the conceptual foundation upon which distributed systems like Spark and Dask are built.

Think of RAM as a small desk and disk as a large filing cabinet in the next room. You cannot spread all 10,000 documents across your desk at once. Instead, you pull out a drawer (chunk), work on those files, put them away, and pull out the next drawer. The total work gets done, your desk never overflows, and you never needed a bigger desk вҖ” just a smarter workflow.

A well-designed out-of-core pipeline is essentially an assembly line: data flows in one end, transformations happen in the middle, and results flow out the other end, with only one batch of data alive in memory at any moment.
: RAM usage stays constant regardless of dataset size, controlled entirely by chunk size.
: No need for expensive high-memory machines for many workloads.
: Naturally compatible with data arriving in real time.
: Chunks are independent and can be processed concurrently.
: While one chunk is being processed, the next can be prefetched from disk.
: Python's generator protocol makes it trivial to chain out-of-core steps into clean pipelines.
: Disk I/O is orders of magnitude slower than RAM access. NVMe SSDs help significantly, but the bottleneck shifts from computation to I/O.
: Not all algorithms admit chunked or streaming versions. Sorting, joins, and aggregations that require global state need careful redesign.
: When data doesn't all exist in memory at once, inspecting intermediate states is less convenient.
: Too small вҶ’ too many I/O operations; too large вҶ’ OOM errors. Finding the right size requires profiling.
: Streaming transformations that need context across chunk boundaries (e.g., rolling windows that span chunks) require explicit bookkeeping.
Out-of-core processing rests on three fundamental ideas:
вҖ” Data is divided into fixed-size blocks. Each block is read into RAM, processed, and discarded before the next is loaded. The chunk size is the primary tuning knob.

вҖ” Rather than materializing a full result set, computation is expressed as a pipeline of lazy transformations. Data is pulled through the pipeline on demand вҖ” no intermediate result ever needs to exist in full.
вҖ” Many aggregations (sum, count, min, max, running average) can be maintained as running statistics updated with each new chunk, without ever accumulating all values.
Python's Generator Protocol as the Foundation
Python generators are the lowest-level building block for out-of-core processing. A generator function uses
to produce values one at a time, meaning it never holds more than one item in memory:
The entire pipeline runs lazily вҖ” no data is read until the terminal consumer (a loop,
, a file write) requests the next item.

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

в”Ӯ             CSV, Parquet, HDF5, JSON Lines, Database            в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ   Yields one chunk at a time вҖ” only N rows in RAM              в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ   Each step is a generator that consumes and yields chunks      в”Ӯ

                         в”Ӯ transformed chunk

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

в”Ӯ   Accumulate running stats  OR  Write chunk to output file     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   Running sum / count / mean updated per chunk                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ   Output: Parquet file, database, model weights, report         в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
at most one chunk lives in RAM at any point in the pipeline
argument that turns it into an iterator of DataFrames:

import pandas as pd

def compute_total_revenue(filepath):
    total = 0.0
    count = 0

    for chunk in pd.read_csv(filepath, chunksize=50_000):
        # Each chunk is a regular DataFrame вҖ” all pandas operations work
        chunk = chunk.dropna(subset=["revenue"])
        chunk["revenue"] = pd.to_numeric(chunk["revenue"], errors="coerce")
        total += chunk["revenue"].sum()
        count += len(chunk)

    return total, count

total, n = compute_total_revenue("sales_2024.csv")
print(f"Total revenue: {total:,.2f} over {n:,} records")
means only 50,000 rows occupy RAM at once. The loop runs as many iterations as needed to exhaust the file.
Never accumulate results in a growing list. Write each processed chunk directly to output:

import pandas as pd

def filter_and_save(input_path, output_path, threshold):
    first_chunk = True

    for chunk in pd.read_csv(input_path, chunksize=50_000):
        filtered = chunk[chunk["score"] > threshold]

        filtered.to_csv(
            output_path,
            mode="w" if first_chunk else "a",   # overwrite first, append after
            header=first_chunk,                   # write header only once
            index=False
        )
        first_chunk = False
3. Using Parquet with PyArrow for Efficient Chunking
Parquet is a columnar binary format that is far more efficient than CSV for large datasets. PyArrow provides native batch iteration:

import pyarrow.parquet as pq
import pyarrow as pa

def process_parquet(input_path, output_path):
    parquet_file = pq.ParquetFile(input_path)
    writer = None

    for batch in parquet_file.iter_batches(batch_size=100_000):
        # batch is a pyarrow RecordBatch вҖ” convert to pandas if needed
        df = batch.to_pandas()
        df = df[df["age"] >= 18]
        result_batch = pa.RecordBatch.from_pandas(df)

        if writer is None:
            writer = pq.ParquetWriter(output_path, result_batch.schema)
        writer.write_batch(result_batch)

    if writer:
        writer.close()
Parquet offers columnar reads вҖ” if only 3 of 50 columns are needed, only those columns are read from disk, massively reducing I/O.
A clean production-style pipeline using chained generators:

import pandas as pd
import csv

# --- Stage 1: Source ---
def csv_chunks(filepath, chunksize=50_000):
    for chunk in pd.read_csv(filepath, chunksize=chunksize):
        yield chunk

# --- Stage 2: Clean ---
def clean(chunks):
    for chunk in chunks:
        chunk = chunk.dropna()
        chunk = chunk[chunk["amount"] > 0]
        yield chunk

# --- Stage 3: Transform ---
def add_features(chunks):
    for chunk in chunks:
        chunk["log_amount"] = chunk["amount"].apply(lambda x: x ** 0.5)
        chunk["month"] = pd.to_datetime(chunk["date"]).dt.month
        yield chunk

# --- Stage 4: Sink (write output) ---
def write_output(chunks, output_path):
    first = True
    for chunk in chunks:
        chunk.to_csv(output_path, mode="w" if first else "a",
                     header=first, index=False)
        first = False

# --- Wire it together ---
def run_pipeline(input_path, output_path):
    pipeline = csv_chunks(input_path)
    pipeline = clean(pipeline)
    pipeline = add_features(pipeline)
    write_output(pipeline, output_path)

run_pipeline("transactions.csv", "clean_transactions.csv")
Each stage is independent, testable, and reusable. The entire pipeline consumes constant memory regardless of file size.
For aggregations like mean and variance across a dataset too large to hold in memory, use online (incremental) algorithms:
This produces exact mean and variance with a single pass through the data and O(1) memory.
HDF5 is a hierarchical binary format designed for large numerical arrays.
supports partial reads with numpy-style slicing:

import h5py
import numpy as np

def process_hdf5(filepath, dataset_name, chunk_size=10_000):
    with h5py.File(filepath, "r") as f:
        dataset = f[dataset_name]
        total_rows = dataset.shape[0]
        result_sum = np.zeros(dataset.shape[1])  # column-wise sum

        for start in range(0, total_rows, chunk_size):
            end = min(start + chunk_size, total_rows)
            chunk = dataset[start:end, :]          # reads only these rows
            result_sum += chunk.sum(axis=0)

    return result_sum
HDF5 datasets support transparent compression and arbitrary-dimensional chunking, making them excellent for numerical scientific workloads.
Real-World Example: Processing 200 GB of Event Logs
: An analytics team has 200 GB of JSON-lines event log files on an NFS mount. The goal is to compute daily active users (DAU) for each day in the past year. The server has 32 GB RAM.
Files are processed one at a time; never all loaded simultaneously.
Records are batched into DataFrames for vectorized
accumulates unique users per day вҖ” for truly massive user counts this would be replaced with a HyperLogLog structure to stay memory-bounded even for the aggregate.
Output is a simple dictionary that can be written to a database or CSV.
For even larger scale, this pipeline would use Dask or PySpark to parallelize across multiple files simultaneously.
: The most common mistake is collecting processed chunks into a list inside the loop, negating all memory savings:

# WRONG вҖ” grows without bound
results = []
for chunk in pd.read_csv("big.csv", chunksize=50_000):
    results.append(process(chunk))       # RAM fills up
final = pd.concat(results)              # OOM here
# RIGHT вҖ” write each chunk immediately
for chunk in pd.read_csv("big.csv", chunksize=50_000):
    process(chunk).to_csv("out.csv", mode="a", header=False, index=False)
: Forgetting to suppress the header on all chunks after the first produces malformed output with header rows embedded in the data.
produces millions of iterations with constant Python overhead per iteration, often making processing slower than a moderate in-memory load.
: Pandas re-infers dtypes on every chunk by default. Specifying
is dramatically faster for large files.
: A rolling 7-day average computed per-chunk will produce wrong results at chunk boundaries. The boundary state (the last 6 days of the previous chunk) must be carried forward explicitly.
: When using CSV, all columns are parsed even if only 2 of 30 are used. Always pass
to avoid unnecessary memory and parsing work.

: CSV is the worst format for large-scale out-of-core work вҖ” no compression, no columnar access, slow to parse. Prefer Parquet (columnar, compressed, schema-preserving) for analytical workloads, or HDF5/Zarr for numerical arrays.
: Start with a chunk size of ~50,000вҖ“100,000 rows for DataFrames or ~64 MBвҖ“256 MB for raw bytes. Profile memory usage with
: Read only the columns needed and specify types explicitly. This can reduce per-chunk memory by 5вҖ“10Г— for wide tables.
: A 10 GB CSV might compress to 800 MB as Parquet with Snappy compression, with 10Г— faster read speeds due to columnar access.
Overlap I/O and compute with threading
to prefetch the next chunk while the current chunk is being processed:
work naturally with generator-based pipelines at zero memory cost.

from tqdm import tqdm
import os

total_size = os.path.getsize("big.csv")
with tqdm(total=total_size, unit="B", unit_scale=True) as pbar:
    for chunk in pd.read_csv("big.csv", chunksize=50_000):
        process(chunk)
        pbar.update(chunk.memory_usage(deep=True).sum())
I/O Bandwidth is the Dominant Bottleneck

For most out-of-core workloads on a single machine, computation is fast and I/O is the bottleneck. A modern NVMe SSD delivers ~3вҖ“7 GB/s sequential read throughput. An HDD delivers ~100вҖ“200 MB/s. Choosing a compressed binary format can turn a 500 MB/s CSV parse into a 3 GB/s Parquet read, effectively making the problem compute-bound instead of I/O-bound.

is the number of pipeline stages that hold a live reference to a chunk simultaneously. A well-designed generator pipeline has depth 1 вҖ” only one chunk exists at any time. A poorly designed one with intermediate materialization may have depth equal to the number of stages.
Out-of-core chunk processing is embarrassingly parallel at the chunk level. Multiple chunks can be processed simultaneously:

from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
import pandas as pd

def process_chunk(chunk_data):
    # Runs in a worker process вҖ” no GIL contention for CPU work
    df = pd.DataFrame(chunk_data)
    return df[df["value"] > 100]["value"].sum()

chunks = list(pd.read_csv("big.csv", chunksize=50_000))

with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=8) as executor:
    results = list(executor.map(process_chunk,
                                 [chunk.to_dict("records") for chunk in chunks]))

total = sum(results)
For true large-scale parallel out-of-core processing, Dask or PySpark manage chunk distribution automatically.

When a single machine is insufficient even with chunking, the natural extension is distributed processing. Dask provides a pandas-compatible API that automatically partitions data across multiple workers:

import dask.dataframe as dd

# Dask reads the file lazily, partitioned into chunks automatically
df = dd.read_csv("s3://my-bucket/big_data/*.csv", blocksize="128MB")

# Operations are lazy вҖ” nothing runs yet
result = df[df["amount"] > 0].groupby("category")["amount"].sum()

# .compute() triggers actual execution across all partitions
final = result.compute()
Dask uses the same chunking strategy under the hood вҖ” it's out-of-core processing automated and distributed.
Simple chunked ETL on a single machine
Efficient Parquet I/O with batch iteration
HDF5 file access with partial reads
Billion-row exploratory analysis on one machine
Fast Rust-backed DataFrame with streaming mode
Embedded SQL engine with disk-based query
Complex SQL queries over large local files
deserves special mention: it can run analytical SQL directly over Parquet files on disk, effectively providing out-of-core SQL without any setup:

import duckdb

# Reads only the needed columns from Parquet, pushes filters to disk
result = duckdb.query("""
    SELECT category, SUM(amount) as total
    FROM read_parquet('large_data.parquet')
    WHERE amount > 0
    GROUP BY category
    ORDER BY total DESC
""").to_df()

Memory-mapped approaches (Vaex, mmap) are a middle ground: the OS maps the file into virtual address space and loads pages on demand. This can be faster than explicit chunking because the OS manages prefetching, but it requires sufficient virtual address space (64-bit OS) and doesn't work over network storage efficiently.
: If a pipeline crashes mid-write, the output file is partially written and corrupt. Mitigate with atomic writes вҖ” write to a temporary file and rename on success:
: For pipelines that run for hours, save the last processed chunk index to a checkpoint file so they can resume after failure:
: Validate schemas and value ranges on each chunk to catch corrupt data early rather than discovering it only in the final output:

def validate_chunk(chunk):
    assert "user_id" in chunk.columns, "Missing user_id column"
    assert chunk["amount"].dtype == float, "amount must be float"
    assert (chunk["amount"] >= 0).all(), "Negative amounts found"
    return chunk
statements) when opening files and writers. For long pipelines,
blocks ensure files are closed even on failure.
Out-of-core processing keeps memory usage constant by processing data in chunks, enabling datasets of arbitrary size to run on ordinary hardware.
Python generators are the native building block: they compose naturally into multi-stage pipelines where only one chunk exists in memory at any time.
partial reads are the three primary tools for chunk-based I/O in Python.
to reduce per-chunk memory and parsing time.
Prefer binary columnar formats (Parquet) over CSV for large datasets вҖ” they offer 5вҖ“20Г— faster reads and 3вҖ“10Г— smaller file sizes.
Never accumulate chunks into a growing list; write results incrementally to the sink.
For parallel out-of-core processing on one machine, use Dask or Polars streaming mode; for distributed scale, use PySpark or Dask distributed.
Protect long-running pipelines with atomic writes and checkpointing to handle failures gracefully.
